channel.title: NYT > U.S. News
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: 5 Notable Moments From Candace Owens’s Warm Interview With Hunter Biden
item.description: In a nearly two-hour conversation, Mr. Biden discussed his addiction to drugs and alcohol, and Ms. Owens apologized for her past remarks about him.
item.dc:creator: Tim Balk

item.title: San Diego Mosque Shooter So Alarmed Police, They Seized Father’s Guns
item.description: In 2025, Caleb Vazquez’s obsession with mass shooters and Nazism prompted reports to the authorities. His father surrendered dozens of firearms.
item.dc:creator: Orlando Mayorquín, Tim Arango and Chelsia Rose Marcius
item.category: Islamic Center of San Diego Shooting (2026)
item.media:credit: John Francis Peters for The New York Times
item.media:description: Families escorted their children across police lines after a shooting at the Islamic Center of San Diego on Monday.

item.title: In San Diego, a Final Prayer Before Laying Heroes to Rest
item.description: Thousands of mourners filled a San Diego park to recite a funeral prayer for the three men killed by gunmen at a mosque on Monday.
item.dc:creator: Orlando Mayorquín
item.category: Islamic Center of San Diego Shooting (2026)
item.media:credit: John Francis Peters for The New York Times
item.media:description: Mourners gathered for a funeral prayer for three men who died on Monday while confronting two shooters at the Islamic Center of San Diego.

item.title: Trump Officials’ Strict Stand on Ebola Leaves Health Experts ‘Stunned’
item.description: Public health experts say the administration’s quarantine orders go beyond what is needed to prevent the U.S. spread of Ebola and hantavirus.
item.dc:creator: Apoorva Mandavilli and Jacey Fortin
item.category: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
item.media:credit: Eric Lee/The New York Times
item.media:description: Jay Bhattacharya, the acting director of the C.D.C., issued quarantine orders for two cruise passengers who wanted to leave the Nebraska facility and isolate in their home states.

item.title: In a Rarity, Republicans Stand Up to Trump
item.description: President Trump faced a wall of opposition from Senate G.O.P. lawmakers, in part over his plan to create a $1.8 billion fund to reward his allies.
item.dc:creator: Luke Broadwater
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: Senator Susan Collins, Republican of Maine, is known to oppose the most extreme parts of President Trump’s agenda.

item.title: Outside Colbert’s Final Show, Fans From Near and Far Line Up for a Farewell
item.description: Hundreds of people, including a pope, staked out the Ed Sullivan Theater in Manhattan, hoping to get in, or just get a glimpse.
item.dc:creator: Rylee Kirk, Alexandra E. Petri and Graham Dickie
item.media:description: The crowd outside the Ed Sullivan Theater on Thursday afternoon.

item.title: Trump to Deploy 5,000 Troops to Poland, Surprising the Pentagon
item.description: Defense Department officials had abruptly canceled the deployments of thousands of troops there just last week.
item.dc:creator: Eric Schmitt and Chris Cameron
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Carolyn Van Houten for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump announced in a social media post on Thursday that the United States would deploy 5,000 troops to Poland.

item.title: GOP Pulls Measure on Trump’s War Powers in Iran, Lacking Votes to Defeat It
item.description: House Republican leaders abruptly scrapped a planned vote on a measure to direct President Trump to end the conflict or win authorization for it, amid party defections and absences.
item.dc:creator: Megan Mineiro, Robert Jimison and Michael Gold
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times

item.title: Judge Appears Hesitant to Order Work Stopped on Reflecting Pool
item.description: Judge Carl J. Nichols seemed skeptical that the changes President Trump has rushed to make would irreversibly deface the pool if he later found them illegal.
item.dc:creator: Zach Montague and David A. Fahrenthold
item.media:credit: Allison Robbert for The New York Times
item.media:description: This lawsuit argued in Thursday’s hearing is focused on the Lincoln Memorial Reflecting Pool’s new color, which Mr. Trump said he chose himself and calls “American flag blue.”

item.title: Trump Settled a Case With Himself. Was That Legal?
item.description: The Trump administration this week created a $1.8 billion fund to dole out taxpayer dollars to the president’s political allies, and declared that Mr. Trump is immunized from tax audits. Our chief legal affairs correspondent, Adam Liptak, explains how these legally questionable moves test the Constitution’s limits on the president’s power.
item.dc:creator: Adam Liptak, Paul Abowd, Nikolay Nikolov, Rafaela Balster, Jon Miller and Whitney Shefte

item.title: Potential 2028 contenders are among those dismayed by the D.N.C. autopsy.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Lerer
item.media:credit: Rachel Wisniewski for The New York Times
item.media:description: Gov. Josh Shapiro of Pennsylvania expressed concern about the handling of a draft report on the Democratic Party’s 2024 presidential election loss.

item.title: U.S. Adds Security Measures at Dulles to Receive Citizens Who Have Been in Ebola Outbreak Region
item.description: The Department of Homeland Security directed all flights carrying certain travelers to arrive at Dulles International Airport in Virginia.
item.dc:creator: Madeleine Ngo
item.category: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
item.media:credit: Aleksey Kondratyev for The New York Times
item.media:description: The government is putting in place “enhanced public health measures” at Dulles International Airport.

item.title: Federal Prosecutors Drop Charges Against ICE Protesters in Chicago Area
item.description: For months, the prosecutors had pursued charges against several people who demonstrated outside an immigration detention facility in Broadview, Ill. On Thursday, the cases were dismissed.
item.dc:creator: Julie Bosman
item.media:credit: Jim Vondruska/Reuters
item.media:description: Federal agents detained protesters outside a detention facility in Broadview, Ill., on Sept. 26.

item.title: Trump Says He Will ‘Try and Make’ Son Don Jr.’s Wedding, but Timing ‘Not Good’
item.description: President Trump said the war with Iran and “other things” would make it difficult for him to make the wedding this weekend.
item.dc:creator: Shawn McCreesh
item.media:credit: Pete Marovich for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump, right, with his son Donald Trump Jr. earlier this month. The younger Mr. Trump’s wedding is this weekend.

item.title: Tennessee Calls Off Execution After Staff Can’t Find Prisoner’s Vein
item.description: Tony Carruthers, convicted in connection with three 1994 murders, was scheduled to be executed Thursday morning.
item.dc:creator: Emily Cochrane and Nicholas Bogel-Burroughs
item.media:credit: Mark Humphrey/Associated Press
item.media:description: Death penalty opponents stood outside the Riverbend Maximum Security Institution before the scheduled execution of Tony Carruthers in Nashville on Thursday.

item.title: 5 Takeaways From the Democrats’ Autopsy of Kamala Harris’s 2024 Loss
item.description: A draft report released by the Democratic National Committee argued that Ms. Harris did not sufficiently separate herself from President Joseph R. Biden Jr.
item.dc:creator: Tim Balk
item.media:credit: Jamie Kelter Davis for The New York Times
item.media:description: Former Vice President Kamala Harris’s 2024 presidential election loss was the focus of a draft report released on Thursday by the Democratic National Committee.

item.title: Even in a crisis, Ken Martin, the D.N.C. chairman, keeps his supporters.
item.dc:creator: Reid J. Epstein
item.media:credit: Annie Mulligan for The New York Times
item.media:description: Ken Martin, the chairman of the Democratic National Committee, has been criticized for his lack of fund-raising, as well as his waffling about whether to release a draft report on the party’s 2024 presidential election loss.

item.title: 2 Florida College Students Suspended Over Racist Group Chat
item.description: The students held leadership positions in Republican groups. The racist rhetoric, documented in court papers, highlights a growing rift in the party.
item.dc:creator: Stephanie Saul
item.media:credit: Martina Tuaty for The New York Times
item.media:description: Florida International University is one of several college campuses where students have been accused of racist and antisemitic behavior in recent months.

item.title: Inside the Senate G.O.P. Meltdown Over Trump’s Fund
item.description: Todd Blanche, the acting attorney general, went to Capitol Hill to allay Republicans’ concerns over a fund to pay people who claim government mistreatment. It did not go well.
item.dc:creator: Michael Gold
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: Senator John Thune, Republican of South Dakota and the majority leader, said his colleagues had “very legitimate questions” about the new fund.

item.title: Federal Panel Approves Trump’s Plans for a 250-Foot Arch in Washington
item.description: The panel, whose members were selected by President Trump, has an advisory role on the design of the project but no enforcement power.
item.dc:creator: Luke Broadwater
item.media:credit: U.S. Commission of Fine Arts
item.media:description: A digital rendering of the proposed 250-foot triumphal arch to be built in Washington. Its future remains uncertain as it faces a legal challenge.

item.title: Here’s the latest.
item.dc:creator: Shane Goldmacher, Reid J. Epstein and Lisa Lerer

item.title: Former Detroit Mayor Duggan Drops Out of Race for Michigan Governor
item.description: Mike Duggan, who had overseen the city after its bankruptcy, was running far behind Jocelyn Benson in polls and fund-raising.
item.dc:creator: Julie Bosman and Mitch Smith
item.media:credit: Nick Hagen for The New York Times
item.media:description: Mike Duggan was the first major candidate to enter the race for governor in Michigan, a closely divided state.

item.title: Justices Decline to Rule in Death Penalty Case Over Intellectual Disabilities
item.description: An Alabama man challenged his death sentence after a murder conviction because of his varying results in a series of I.Q. tests.
item.dc:creator: Ann E. Marimow
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: The Alabama case also raised the issue of the extent to which states must consider a broader evaluation of evidence beyond I.Q. test scores in deciding if a person is disabled.

item.title: Trump’s $1.8 Billion Fund in the Cross Hairs
item.description: Critics say the deal creates a “slush fund,” a term with a colorful maritime history.
item.dc:creator: Adam Liptak
item.media:credit: London Stereoscopic Company/Hulton Archive — Getty Images
item.media:description: Barrels on the Royal Albert Docks in London in the 19th century.

item.title: Republicans Delay Budget Votes as They Balk at Trump’s Fund
item.description: The G.O.P. abandoned its plan to quickly pass a $72 billion immigration crackdown bill as senators revolted over his plan to pay people who claim government persecution.
item.dc:creator: Michael Gold and Carl Hulse
item.category: White House East Wing and Ballroom (Washington, DC)
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: Senator John Thune, Republican of South Dakota and the majority leader, scrapped plans for votes on Thursday and lawmakers left for a weeklong recess.

item.title: D.N.C. 2024 Election Autopsy Reopens Wounds of Harris Loss
item.description: The report partly blamed aides to Joseph R. Biden Jr. and Kamala Harris for her defeat. But it was widely seen as a poorly executed examination, bringing fresh scrutiny to Democrats.
item.dc:creator: Shane Goldmacher, Reid J. Epstein and Lisa Lerer
item.media:credit: Ruth Fremson/The New York Times
item.media:description: Vice President Kamala Harris at the 2024 Democratic National Convention. A draft report released on Thursday criticized the Harris campaign for failing to distance itself from President Joseph R. Biden Jr.

item.title: 2 Minnesota Autism Therapy Providers Charged in $46 Million Medicaid Fraud Case
item.description: The Justice Department claims that clinics used fake diagnoses and kickbacks to parents to bring children into treatment.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Kliff and Ernesto Londoño
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump focused attention on fraud in Minnesota after news reports and a social media video from a conservative content creator last year.

item.title: Charges Dismissed for Ex-Assistant Principal After Shooting by 6-Year-Old
item.description: Prosecutors had argued that Ebony Parker, a former assistant principal at a Virginia elementary school, had failed to act on warnings that a child had a gun in his backpack.
item.dc:creator: Chris Hippensteel and Jackson Landers
item.media:credit: Billy Schuerman/The Virginian-Pilot, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Richneck Elementary School in Newport News, Va., as students returned following a shooting at the school in 2023.

item.title: Republicans Seize on a Budget Exemption to Push Through Immigration Enforcement Funding
item.description: Republicans are using a special mechanism that was created to reduce deficits to push through immigration enforcement funds that should be provided in a regular spending bill.
item.dc:creator: Carl Hulse
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: Senate Republicans are muscling through legislation to provide more than $70 billion by employing a special budget exemption that was never intended to be used this way.

item.title: Supreme Court Permits Lawsuit Over U.S. Assets Seized by Cuba in 1960
item.description: The Trump administration backed a lawsuit brought by the Havana Docks Corporation that would allow the U.S.-owned entity to get compensation for property confiscated by Fidel Castro’s regime.
item.dc:creator: Ann E. Marimow
item.media:credit: Havana Docks Company
item.media:description: Cuban revolutionaries taking over the Havana Docks Corporation facilities in 1960.

item.title: Fan A.I. Videos Help Spencer Pratt Break Through in L.A. Mayor’s Race
item.description: Supporters have created A.I. videos to boost the mayoral campaign of Spencer Pratt, the former MTV star. Some videos have gone viral, but it’s not clear whether they will yield votes.
item.dc:creator: Madison Malone Kircher and Ken Bensinger
item.media:credit: Gabriela Bhaskar/The New York Times
item.media:description: Spencer Pratt’s campaign has received a boost from A.I. videos that depict him as a superhero fighting Democratic leaders.

item.title: Bone Found Near Guthrie Home Predates Her Disappearance by 750 Years
item.description: Archaeological experts say the discovery is a cautionary tale about the consequences of citizen investigators taking matters into their own hands.
item.dc:creator: Reis Thebault
item.media:credit: Rebecca Noble/Reuters
item.media:description: Journalists gathered near the home of Nancy Guthrie after she went missing in Tucson, Ariz., in February.

item.title: Trump’s Fund Shows Blanche Choosing Loyalty Over Pushing Back
item.description: Once seen by some as the most conventional of President Trump’s political appointees, Todd Blanche has taken off the gloves in his new role as acting attorney general.
item.dc:creator: Glenn Thrush
item.category: House Select Committee to Investigate the January 6th Attack
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: Todd Blanche, the acting attorney general, during an appearance before the Senate appropriations subcommittee on Tuesday.

item.title: ‘We’ve Never Been This Bad.’ Eastern Pennsylvania Weighs Rising Costs.
item.description: With two swing districts, the region from Scranton to Allentown could decide control of the House, and voters are expressing profound economic distress.
item.dc:creator: Sabrina Tavernise and Hannah Beier
item.media:credit: Hannah Beier for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Lehigh Valley in Pennsylvania includes midsize cities like Allentown that are growing increasingly diverse.

item.title: Colony Ridge Developer Sues Alex Jones for Defamation Over ‘Sanctuary City’ Claims in Texas
item.description: The suit in Texas was an attempt by the owners of the development known as Colony Ridge to repair its the image after years of Republican and Democratic attacks.
item.dc:creator: J. David Goodman
item.media:credit: Danielle Villasana for The New York Times
item.media:description: A home under construction in Colony Ridge, a quickly growing community near Houston, in 2023.

item.title: Democrats’ Midterm Strength Masks Fierce Divides and Frustration, Poll Shows
item.description: Democratic voters are in a combative, anti-establishment mood, unhappy with their party and disagreeing about its best path forward, a New York Times/Siena poll found.
item.dc:creator: Katie Glueck, Ruth Igielnik and Camille Baker
item.media:credit: Karl Russell

item.title: What Graduation Speakers Are Telling the Class of 2026
item.description: Will A.I. replace them? Graduation speakers addressed student uncertainty about the future with jokes and words of wisdom.
item.dc:creator: Emma Schartz
item.category: North Carolina Agricultural and Technical State University
Rutgers, The State University of New Jersey
item.media:credit: Daniel Jacobi II/The Daily Hampshire Gazette, via AP
item.media:description: The Class of 2026 faces a tough labor market, A.I. disruption and political division as they head out into the world. Celebrities and academics who spoke at commencement ceremonies had plenty of advice to help them.
